data.title: Exxon says FPSO for fifth Guyana offshore oil and gas project 'almost complete'
data.description:
A new floating production facility for the Exxon Mobil-led (XOM) consortium in Guyana is "almost complete" and expected ‌to soon depart from Singapore, the company's head in Guyana, Alistair Routledge, said Thursday, according to Reuters.

The Errea Wittu floating ⁠production, storage and offloading platform is the ​fifth to be installed by the Exxon-led (XOM) group in Guyana, and will produce, store and ​deliver as much as 250K bbl/day from ​the Uaru offshore project.

Exxon (XOM) had said previously that the FPSO would ⁠arrive in Guyanese waters this year, without further details.

Following ​Uaru, ⁠Exxon (XOM) is on track to start the Whiptail project by the end of 2027, while ⁠trying to ​accelerate the startup of ​the Hammerhead project for 2028, Routledge said.

Separately, a joint venture by Exxon (XOM), Energean, and Helleniq Energy has notified Greece it plans to look for gas in a ​western offshore block, the country's hydrocarbons management ​agency said Thursday, Reuters reported.

After assessing the area's potential, the JV notified ​the agency of its plans to proceed to the second phase, ‌which ⁠includes the first offshore exploration drilling in 40 years, the state-owned agency said.

data.title: Intuitive Machines outlines $900M-$1B 2026 revenue target as Lanteris acquisition drives diversification
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intuitive Machines (LUNR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Altemus described 2025 as a transformational year, highlighting the completion of the company’s second lunar mission, expansion into national security space programs, the acquisition of KinetX Aerospace, and the announced acquisition of Lanteris Space Systems. Altemus stated, "we expect 2026 revenue to approach $1 billion, nearly a 5x increase from 2025," and detailed a diversified revenue mix moving toward 40% commercial, 40% civil space, and 20% national security customers.
  • Altemus emphasized the company’s Moon-first infrastructure strategy and detailed progression in low Earth orbit, geostationary, and lunar domains. He highlighted the launch of EchoStar XXV, upcoming SiriusXM satellite deliveries, and the near-completion of NASA’s lunar Gateway Power and Propulsion Element.
  • Altemus noted the integration of Lanteris brings proven satellite production platforms to new growth markets, stating the company is “focused on taking proven production platforms and applying them to new growth markets as a prime operator.”
  • CFO Peter McGrath stated, "Q4 2025 revenue was $44.8 million, driven primarily by CLPS, OMES and NSNS execution... Q4 gross margin came in strong at $8.5 million, which represents a 19% positive gross margin." McGrath also highlighted the completion of the Lanteris acquisition in January and a $175 million strategic equity investment to advance communications data processing networks.

Outlook

  • McGrath stated, "For 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $900 million to $1 billion, representing a transformational step-up in scale for the company. Importantly, roughly 2/3 of our expected 2026 revenue is already supported by contracted backlog, giving us strong visibility into our outlook."
  • The company is targeting positive adjusted EBITDA for 2026 driven by scale from the Lanteris acquisition, growth in higher-margin service revenue, and operational efficiencies.
  • Management noted that the backlog as of February was $943 million, with additional awards pending that could further increase visibility.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 revenue was $44.8 million, primarily from CLPS, OMES, and NSNS programs. Gross margin for the quarter was $8.5 million or 19%.
  • OMES revenue in Q4 was $14.7 million. Operating loss for the quarter was $33.1 million, compared to a loss of $13.4 million in Q4 2024, due mainly to acquisition-related expenses and continued investment in growth.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was negative $19.1 million in Q4. Operating cash used was $7.3 million, with capital expenditures of $15.6 million, resulting in negative free cash flow of $22.9 million. Year-end cash balance was $583 million, with $403 million subsequently used for the Lanteris acquisition.
  • As of the end of February, cash was $272 million following the acquisition and capital raise. Year-end backlog was $213.1 million, and combined company backlog reached $943 million by February end.

Q&A

  • Joshua Sullivan, JonesTrading: Asked about Lanteris integration and customer response. Altemus responded integration is "going very well," with transition activities "well ahead of schedule."
  • Sujeeva De Silva, ROTH Capital: Inquired about national security growth and programs. Altemus discussed recent Space Development Agency Tranche 3 win and national security pipeline, including Golden Dome and orbital transfer vehicle development.
  • De Silva also questioned 2026 revenue linearity and upside. McGrath described revenue as "pretty level throughout the year" and noted potential upside if Artemis missions accelerate.
  • Andres Sheppard-Slinger, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about new capabilities from the Lanteris acquisition and LTV program award structure. Altemus described expanded opportunities and imminent LTV decision, noting, "we feel it's imminent."
  • Austin Moeller, Canaccord: Sought details on operational changes at Lanteris. Altemus credited Chris Johnson for streamlining and investing in the 300 series, stating the business is "strong and producing."
  • Xin Yu, Deutsche Bank: Asked about architecture for space data centers. Altemus said, "I think edge computing in space and making decision -- doing decision-making in space is the key to the future of data centers as opposed to replacing terrestrial-based data centers."
  • Jonathan Siegmann, Stifel: Queried on LTV timing amid Artemis restructuring. Altemus indicated independence from Artemis schedule, stating, "we're not tied to the sequence of events for Artemis V."
  • Samantha Stiroh, BofA: Asked about the impact of the Space Superiority executive order. Altemus said the company is "working directly with NASA to look at ways to move efforts forward faster."
  • Griffin Boss, B. Riley: Questioned CLPS 2.0 scale. Altemus expects CLPS 2.0 "to be larger than CLPS 1" and highlighted a push for higher cadence and heavier cargo missions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on integration execution, revenue growth potential, and national security pipeline, with a generally positive and congratulatory tone, seeking additional clarity on growth levers and program awards.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, frequently using phrases like "very excited," "well ahead of schedule," and "the future is very bright," while addressing program risks and operational execution in a straightforward manner.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analyst sentiment shifted further toward optimism, with management exhibiting higher confidence due to acquisition closure and improved financial visibility, and analysts expressing more interest in near-term catalysts and integration progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance for 2026 revenue ($900 million to $1 billion) was provided, compared to previous quarter’s focus on acquisition rationale and strategic positioning without specific revenue guidance.
  • The revenue mix evolved from a 25% defense/25% civil/50% commercial split to an anticipated 40% commercial/40% civil/20% national security blend, reflecting a more balanced and diversified portfolio.
  • The Lanteris acquisition moved from announcement (Q3) to completion (Q4), with integration and customer reception now in focus.
  • Gross margin improved in Q4 versus Q3, with higher service revenue and cost reductions noted.
  • Management tone became more assertive regarding growth and operational execution, while analysts increased focus on concrete integration outcomes and program wins.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited government budget delays and shutdown as impacting program timing and backlog.
  • Integration challenges for Lanteris were noted, though described as ahead of schedule.
  • Analysts questioned the timing of major program awards, competitive landscape amid Artemis restructuring, and the pace of adoption for new service models.
  • Management identified global competition, bandwidth constraints in deep space communications, and the need for continued investment in infrastructure as ongoing challenges.

Final Takeaway

Intuitive Machines leadership highlighted a year of transformation, marked by major acquisitions and portfolio diversification, projecting revenue of $900 million to $1 billion for 2026 and targeting positive adjusted EBITDA. The completion and integration of Lanteris Space Systems positions the company for expanded opportunities across commercial, civil, and national security markets, with a growing backlog and substantial visibility into future revenue. Management maintains strong confidence in execution, supported by high-margin service growth, robust contract awards, and active pursuit of infrastructure-based recurring revenue programs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: CRWD, TME, UBER, ARM
data.description:
CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Arm Holdings (ARM) have both been upgraded to Hold as analysts point to resilient growth drivers, including strong enterprise demand for cybersecurity products and persistent royalty revenue from smartphone applications. Meanwhile, Tencent Music (TME) and Uber (UBER) face downgrades amid concerns over reduced reporting transparency and competitive threats from autonomous vehicles.

Upgrades

  • CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD): Upgrade to Hold by The Asian Investor. The analyst cites a strong fourth fiscal quarter with record customer acquisition and robust enterprise product uptake, alongside growing AI product adoption driving annual recurring revenue growth.

“I like that CrowdStrike’s ARR business is soaring and that the SaaS enterprise is growing its retention rates. ARR growth and higher customer retention are the two main reasons why I am upgrading CRWD shares from Sell to Hold.”

  • Arm Holdings (ARM): Upgrade to Hold by Jia Ming Eow. Despite concerns in the smartphone sector, the analyst highlights resilient royalty growth from v9 and CSS architectures, along with potential AI chip development opportunities with Nvidia and MediaTek.

“I think that ARM is positioning itself to capitalize on the next phase of AI innovations, with already existing relationships with the largest AI firms like Nvidia. I doubt that ARM will be written off anytime soon despite major fears surrounding their core segments, which have actually shown no signs of a slowdown just yet.”

Downgrades

  • Tencent Music Entertainment Group (TME): Downgrade Buy to Hold by The Value Pendulum. While the company delivered faster revenue growth and wider gross margins in Q4 2025, the analyst expresses concern over a reporting framework shift that reduces data transparency amid intensifying competition.

“The stock's P/E de-rated from 25.1x on Oct 1, 2025 to 18.2x on the last trading day of the year. But it didn't do any repurchases in 4Q25 to take advantage of the cheaper shares. This sends a clear message to me that its operations could be facing stiff competition for a longer-than-expected time.”

  • Uber Technologies (UBER): Downgrade to Hold by PropNotes. The analyst ends a long-standing bullish view, citing slowing revenue growth, potentially non-recurring margin expansion, and mounting competitive threats from autonomous vehicles.

“I believe the company is running out of road, especially on pricing. In other words, I believe there is a ceiling at which the Uber take rate no longer makes sense. Users will coordinate private transfers with local taxis, and drivers will no longer make enough to cover modest wages, depreciation, gas, and other vehicle expenses.”

data.title: DirecTV files suit to block Nexstar-Tegna deal on antitrust grounds
data.description:
DirecTV filed a lawsuit to block Nexstar Media's (NXST) $6.2 billion purchase of Tegna (TGNA) on antitrust grounds.

The lawsuit was filed in  U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of California, Sacramento Division, according to a statement on Thursday.

The DirecTV suit comes as eight states, including California, also filed a suit to block the broadcaster deal. 

"DIRECTV supports the action taken by the states and has determined it is necessary to join this effort to protect competition and consumers," said Michael Hartman, general counsel and chief external affairs officer at DIRECTV said in the statement. "We have consistently made clear that this merger is anti-competitive and not in the public interest and, if it goes forward, will trigger a wave of similar consolidation."

Shares of Tegna fell 1.2%, while Nexstar dropped 1.6%.

data.title: Twilio may benefit from AI as voice and product strategy improve: Needham
data.description:
Twilio (TWLO) was in the spotlight on Thursday as investment firm Needham said the communications software company may be a beneficiary of artificial intelligence as voice and its product strategy improve. 

“We believe a core reason Twilio can drive growth within its voice segment is due to its open ecosystem strategy,” analyst Joshua Reilly wrote in a note to clients. “Eliminating data silos enhances customer experience. A key reason Twilio's CPaaS platform wins at high rates is due to its strong data integration through eliminating data silos. Early innings of latency improvements for Voice AI use cases; should lead to expansion in use cases. We are currently in the early innings of latency improvements for Voice AI use cases, and as latency improves, we believe the use case growth will be exponential.”

Reilly, who has a Buy rating and $145 price target on Twilio shares, added that Twilio has assembled “impressive innovation and go-to-market engines” in its core communications-platform-as-a-service business.

“While the CPaaS market is maturing and has slowed in growth exiting the pull-forward demand from the pandemic, new use cases and market verticals make TWLO confident in achieving 12%+ topline growth with the potential to exceed DD% with strong execution,” Reilly added. “We think that TWLO's global leadership in developer-led sales initiatives, plus a very deep technical bench from which to build AI solutions, differentiates the company from peers and positions the company for higher-margin solutions. Despite global competitors and gross margin pressure on large messaging deals, we believe TWLO can leverage innovation and strong customer relationships to skirt price wars in large, mature deals.”

data.title: Accelerant outlines $5.1B premium target for 2026 while accelerating AI-driven platform shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Accelerant Holdings (ARX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jeffrey Radke, Co-Founder, CEO & Director, stated that "We had a fantastic quarter, beating our expectations on exchange written premium, third-party premium and adjusted EBITDA. All of these figures were in line with our pre-released results." He highlighted Accelerant’s growing AI-driven architecture, emphasizing, "AI is the architecture of our business. It is embedded in how we operate and how we win." Radke detailed three structural advantages: proprietary usable data, algorithm-supported underwriting, and technologically adaptive distribution through MGAs.
  • Radke described the proprietary data set as the company's core moat, noting, "We have assembled 134 million rows of specialty insurance data across 58,000 unique attributes. All of it is proprietary." He emphasized the value of a closed feedback loop for underwriting and claims, which is rare in the industry.
  • The company continues to advance automation with AI, with Radke stating, "Underwriting is becoming increasingly autonomous. We've been building towards that shift for years."
  • Radke shared that exchange written premium reached $1.1 billion for the quarter, with 15 new member additions, totaling 280 by year-end 2025. Net revenue retention was 126%. Gross loss ratio for Q4 was 51%. Third-party direct written premium increased to 40% of exchange written premium.
  • Radke announced key management changes, welcoming Cliff Jenks as incoming General Counsel and Ray Iardella as the new Head of Investor Relations. He also announced CFO Jay Green's departure and Linda Huber's appointment as CFO effective March 31.
  • Jay Green, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "That resulted in revenue of $248 million, which grew 30% year-over-year." Green highlighted, "Adjusted EBITDA was $71 million, growing 52% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA margin rose to 28%, up from 24% last year." He noted more than $2 million in nonrecurring investment gains and a continued shift toward fee-based segments.

Outlook

  • Green guided for Q1 2026 exchange written premium of $1.07 billion to $1.13 billion and third-party direct written premium of $450 million to $470 million. Adjusted EBITDA is expected in the range of $64 million to $66 million.
  • For full year 2026, Green stated, "we expect exchange written premium of at least $5.1 billion and third-party direct written premium of at least $2.2 billion." Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be "at least $275 million."
  • Management reiterated a continued shift toward third-party premium, with a medium-term goal for third-party insurance companies to represent about two-thirds of exchange written premium.

Financial Results

  • Exchange written premium was $1.1 billion, increasing 24% year-over-year. Excluding a terminated low-margin member, year-over-year growth would have been 32%.
  • Revenue for the quarter was $248 million, up 30% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA reached $71 million, with a margin of 28%.
  • Adjusted net income for Q4 2025 was $51 million, or $0.23 per share. The company reported $524 million of cash outside the Underwriting segment, $121 million in outstanding debt, and $633 million of equity capital within Underwriting.
  • The Exchange Services segment delivered $93 million in revenue with a take rate expansion to 8.4%. MGA Operations revenue, excluding nonrecurring gains, was $57 million. Underwriting segment revenue was $111 million, with a margin consistent with prior quarters.

Q&A

  • Rowland Mayor, RBC Capital Markets, asked about adjusted EBITDA upside. Jay Green responded, "we really saw contribution from all 3 segments... we saw really strong performance across the board."
  • Mayor inquired about premium growth guidance and the impact of an MGA cancellation. Ryan Schiller explained the member was "around USD 50 million of premium per quarter... that is impacting Q1 and should also impact Q2 of this year."
  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo, asked about the Hadron contribution and member count outlook. Radke confirmed guidance for Hadron's share and Schiller indicated a robust pipeline for member additions.
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO, focused on cash flow trajectory and the buyback program. Schiller noted, "our cash flow conversion continues to improve," and Radke stated share repurchases are compelling at current levels.
  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs, queried about the risk of MGAs going direct and digital. Radke responded, "Were it to be the case, that would be great news for Accelerant and its members."
  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler, asked about captive expansion. Radke said, "in 2026, the captive book to contribute over $100 million of premium," targeting small to medium-sized clients.
  • Craig Maurer, FT Partners, inquired about net retention ratio. Radke explained, "the vast majority of our net retention is now driven by different jurisdictions, regulatory minimums... we'll be around but slightly below 10%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions on premium growth, member pipeline, cash flow, and strategic shifts, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and positive tone during prepared remarks, frequently using phrases like "we feel really good" and "we are executing extremely well." Responses in the Q&A were direct and detailed, with Radke and Green often emphasizing operational momentum and strategic alignment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed slightly more confidence, with management highlighting progress on third-party insurer diversification and data-driven automation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Exchange written premium increased from $1.04 billion in Q3 to $1.1 billion in Q4.
  • Member count grew from 265 to 280.
  • Third-party direct written premium rose from 32% to 40% of exchange written premium.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin decreased from 39% in Q3 to 28% in Q4; however, nonrecurring gains impacted both quarters.
  • Net retention was 9% in Q4 versus 7% in Q3.
  • Management tone was consistently positive, with greater emphasis on AI as a strategic differentiator in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the complexity of building proprietary data infrastructure and industry challenges in data synthesis.
  • Analysts asked about reserve additions and regulatory minimums affecting net retention.
  • Management described the ongoing shift to third-party insurers and regulatory constraints as natural limits to further reducing net retention.
  • No new material risks were introduced, but ongoing focus remains on underwriting discipline and capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

Accelerant’s management reinforced the company’s structural AI and data-driven advantages, targeting at least $5.1 billion in exchange written premium and $275 million in adjusted EBITDA for 2026. The business continues to shift toward a capital-light, fee-based model, with a growing proportion of third-party premium and strong member growth. Recent management changes and a new share repurchase program reflect confidence in the company’s trajectory and balance sheet, while operational momentum and robust data infrastructure position Accelerant for continued profitable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top Quant bank stocks as Fed proposes new bank capital
data.description:
As the Federal Reserve proposed three new capital rules for banks aimed at making the regulatory regime more efficient and keeping U.S. banks competitive, investors may be looking at top-rated banking stocks using Seeking Alpha’s Quant metrics.

Leaders in the screen include StoneX Group Inc. (SNEX), Galaxy Digital Inc. (GLXY), and The PNC Financial Services Group, Inc. (PNC).

The following list identifies “Strong Buy” and “Buy” opportunities within the financial sector. The screener captures stocks across various banking subsectors, including regional banks, diversified banks, investment banking and brokerage, and asset management and custody banks.

The Federal Reserve, working with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp., introduced three proposals on Thursday.

Two apply to the nation’s largest banks—the Basel III framework and the Global Systemically Important Bank (G-SIB) surcharge—which “streamline the risk-based capital framework using a single set of calculations, improve alignment between requirements and risk, and revise the G-SIB surcharge to better capture the risks of our largest and most complex banks,” according to Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman. The third proposal updates capital requirements for smaller and less complex banks to better align requirements with risks of traditional lending.

Despite the regulatory clarity, the market’s immediate reaction was muted. The KBW Nasdaq Bank Index (BKX) slipped 0.5% on midday Thursday, with most megabanks trading in the red.

Here are the top-rated bank stocks by Quant:

  1. StoneX Group Inc. (SNEX) – SA Quant rating: 4.91, YTD performance: 9.84%
  2. Galaxy Digital Inc. (GLXY) – SA Quant rating: 4.77, YTD performance: -3.49%

  4. State Street Corporation (STT) – SA Quant rating: 4.63, YTD performance: -6.14%
  5. Popular, Inc. (BPOP) – SA Quant rating: 4.60, YTD performance: 3.53%
  6. Bank of Hawaii Corporation (BOH) – SA Quant rating: 4.59, YTD performance: 4.67%
  7. First Horizon Corporation (FHN) – SA Quant rating: 4.58, YTD performance: -8.91%
  8. BankUnited, Inc. (BKU) – SA Quant rating: 4.57, YTD performance: -4.46%
  9. United Bankshares, Inc. (UBSI) – SA Quant rating: 4.57, YTD performance: 1.77%
  10. Fulton Financial Corporation (FULT) – SA Quant rating: 4.55, YTD performance: -0.57%
  11. Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) – SA Quant rating: 4.54, YTD performance: -3.05%
  12. Customers Bancorp, Inc. (CUBI) – SA Quant rating: 4.53, YTD performance: -10.39%
  13. Texas Capital Bancshares, Inc. (TCBI) – SA Quant rating: 4.45, YTD performance: 0.60%
  14. United Community Banks, Inc. (UCB) – SA Quant rating: 4.42, YTD performance: -4.84%
  15. Axos Financial, Inc. (AX) – SA Quant rating: 4.38, YTD performance: -5.08%

data.title: Solo Brands outlines further cost cuts and product innovation for 2026 following $9.6M Q4 adjusted EBITDA turnaround
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Solo Brands, Inc. (SBDS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Larson, President and CEO, stated, "Since stepping into the CEO role in 2025... we have focused on executing a product-led turnaround while building a structurally leaner, profit-driven business." Larson highlighted a comprehensive refinancing, the reinstatement of the New York Stock Exchange listing, and a new ticker symbol as key 2025 milestones. He detailed the consolidation of operations, a more disciplined approach to marketing and pricing at Solo Stove, and a 30%+ reduction in run-rate SG&A, with further actions planned in 2026. Larson also reported that Chubbies achieved over 9% year-over-year growth and emphasized the introduction of five new products and positive operating cash flow for three consecutive quarters. "We generated roughly $19 million of adjusted EBITDA for the year and delivered a 52% increase in fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA, underscoring the operating leverage in our model as these changes take hold," Larson said. The CEO also announced the launch of Cheekies, a women’s swim brand, as part of their product innovation efforts.
  • Laura Coffey, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "In December, we simplified our organizational structure by eliminating the Up-C structure... We believe this streamlined structure is more attractive to investors and enhances good corporate governance." Coffey emphasized, "Consolidated sales were $94 million, down 34.5% versus the prior year quarter... Adjusted gross margin for the fourth quarter was 61%, flat versus a year ago and up by 40 basis points from quarter 3." She also pointed out a 38.8% year-over-year reduction in SG&A expenses in Q4 and noted, "Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was positive $9.6 million or 10.2% of sales. This represented a significant 52% year-over-year improvement and a reversal of the negative EBITDA reported in the third quarter."

Outlook

  • John Larson indicated a continued focus on profitability at the channel, market, and product level for 2026, with ongoing investments in innovation across Solo Stove and Chubbies. Larson stated, "We plan to continue to invest in innovation across Solo Stove and Chubbies and have expanded our watersports assortment through our strategic partnership with Costco." The company is also pursuing international opportunities and plans further cost reductions, especially in payroll, and additional SG&A actions in 2026.
  • Coffey noted, "This year, we expect to invest approximately $3 million to $4 million in growth capital, primarily focused on new product innovation that supports our long-term strategic priorities."

Financial Results

  • Coffey reported consolidated sales of $94 million for the fourth quarter, a decrease of 34.5% year-over-year, but sales were seasonally higher than Q3. Adjusted gross margin was 61%, and SG&A expenses fell by 38.8% year-over-year. Net loss for Q4 was $83.2 million, primarily due to $75.5 million in restructuring and impairment charges. Non-GAAP adjusted net income was $2.3 million, flat year-over-year, but a significant improvement from Q3’s adjusted net loss of $11.9 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $9.6 million, a 52% year-over-year increase. Operating cash flow remained positive for the third consecutive quarter, totaling $28.6 million over the last three quarters. Inventory balances were reduced by nearly 25% year-over-year, and the company ended the year with $20 million in cash and cash equivalents.

Q&A

  • William Hamilton, Kestrel Merchant Partners: Congrats on the profit improvements. I was wondering if you could give us a sense as to like how the categories performed across your different brands in the fourth quarter, just so we maybe could get a sense as to market share changes for the Solo Brands. John Larson responded that Solo Stove’s category was "pretty flat," but market share on units was down due to low-end competition, though at a higher average order value. Chubbies saw market share gains from new product introductions.
  • William Hamilton, Kestrel Merchant Partners: Maybe you could elaborate a little bit more as to how [new products] performed in the quarter and for your thoughts on '26. John Larson answered that new products made up roughly 25% of DTC sales in Q4 and six of the eight top-selling SKUs were recent launches. Larson stressed the importance of selling accessories and moving customers into adjacent categories.
  • William Hamilton, Kestrel Merchant Partners: Just on OpEx... how much restructuring or cost cutting is left and if you could give us some color on that? John Larson replied, "We are definitely looking at cost reduction in the coming year. We haven't come out with exact numbers, but structurally, we're taking a significant amount out in payroll, just as we did last year. I believe in Q4, payroll is down about 27% year-over-year."
  • Mitchell Sacks, Grand Slam Asset Management, LLC: Can you just kind of talk about what your concerns are in '26 and what opportunities you see and what kind of the risks are? John Larson said risks include consumer market volatility and challenges in stemming the revenue decline in Solo Stove. Larson said, "We are set up to flow through, any revenue gains will flow right through the bottom line very efficiently and in the cash flow because we've reduced our cost structure so dramatically."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on market share, new product performance, and the sustainability of cost reductions, indicating a cautiously optimistic tone but seeking clarity about long-term growth and category dynamics. Questions reflected ongoing concerns about competition, OpEx cuts, and revenue stabilization.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing structural transformation and operational improvements. In Q&A, Larson acknowledged market risks and consumer uncertainty but reiterated the company's lean structure and focus on profitability. Phrases such as "We are definitely looking at cost reduction in the coming year" and "We feel the reception has been fairly strong" highlighted both caution and confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone is more upbeat due to improved cash flow and EBITDA, while analysts remain focused on the depth and sustainability of the transformation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured increased emphasis on new product launches and a successful turnaround in adjusted EBITDA from a Q3 loss to a Q4 gain. Guidance language shifted from addressing stabilization to highlighting operational efficiency and product innovation. Payroll and SG&A reductions accelerated, and inventory management tightened further.
  • Analysts continued to probe on product performance and cost discipline, but with greater attention on category-level market share and the impact of new products.
  • Management’s confidence in operational improvements and innovation was more pronounced versus Q3, while still noting risks in the consumer environment and competition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited risks from consumer market uncertainty and geopolitical developments. Larson pointed out, "There's a little bit of risk with the consumer market. We're not sure exactly what's going to happen with what's going on geopolitically in the world."
  • Challenges remain in stemming the revenue decline within Solo Stove, especially with increased low-end competition. Management is mitigating these risks through aggressive product innovation, cost reduction, and expanding into adjacent product categories.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about the sustainability of cost reductions and the competitive landscape.

Final Takeaway

Solo Brands delivered three consecutive quarters of positive operating cash flow and achieved a notable turnaround in adjusted EBITDA in the fourth quarter, driven by aggressive cost reductions, product innovation, and operational discipline. The company remains focused on structural efficiency for 2026, with continued investments in new products, further SG&A and payroll cuts planned, and a disciplined approach to profitability at every level. Management acknowledged remaining risks in consumer demand and competition but emphasized that the foundation for sustainable, long-term growth has been strengthened through recent transformation efforts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tandem gains as Truist becomes latest to upgrade on new pharmacy model
data.description:
Shares of Tandem Diabetes Care (TNDM) rose on Thursday as Truist upgraded the insulin pump maker to Buy from Hold, becoming the latest brokerage to raise its rating based on the company’s new pay-as-you-go pharmacy channel strategy.

On Tuesday, Piper Sandler upgraded the San Diego, California-based MedTech to Overweight from Neutral, arguing that the economic gains of the new strategy will improve Tandem’s (TNDM) revenue figures over the next three years.

In a research note on Thursday, Truist analyst Richard Newitter argued that Wall Street has yet to fully appreciate the growth trajectory of the company’s higher-margin recurring revenue as it transitions to the new model.

While analysts expect TNDM to face headwinds in 2026/27 as it pivots to the new model, which, unlike sales made through the DME channel, carries no upfront payments, he added that “we far prefer the economics of the Pay/GO model in the mid-term.”

Trading at ~1.3x in terms of 2027 revenue, the stock represents a compelling entry point as the Street underestimates the company’s growth and profitability prospects associated with converting patients to the new business model in 2027 and beyond, Newitter added.

He raised his price target on Tandem (TNDM) to $35 from $27 per share.

data.title: Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac hit 52-week low as investors reportedly doubt IPO plans
data.description:
Investors are doubtful about the Trump administration's efforts to sell more stock in Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) to the public, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday.

The U.S. president was looking to offload about 5% of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac in a public offering in September last year.

But few concrete details have emerged since, and attention has seemingly shifted to other priorities, Bloomberg noted.

Both stocks have lost ~70% in the past six months, hitting their 52-week low today.

FNMA was down 7.99% to $4.26 during Thursday late morning, while FMCC was -8.37% to $3.72.
